title: MEA Recording of Electrical Activity in Brain Organoids
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dv4h9","text":"This protocol describes recording spontaneous and pharmacologically modulated activity from brain organoids using a high-density 3D MEA system. Adapt electrode layout, chip type, and software settings to your specific MEA platform. Work gently when transferring organoids to avoid mechanical stress.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: This protocol describes recording spontaneous and pharmacologically modulated activity from brain organoids using a high-density 3D MEA system. Adapt electrode layout, chip type, and software settings to your specific MEA platform. Work quickly but gently when transferring organoids to avoid mechanical stress.

Change organoid culture medium ~48 hours before recording to stabilize activity. Pre-equilibrate MEA chips and recording chamber with warm culture medium. Turn on the MEA system, heating, and gas control (if available) and allow to stabilize at 37 °C .

Using a wide-bore pipette tip, gently transfer individual organoids into the MEA recording chamber. Position each organoid so that it contacts the electrode field (for 3D MEAs, ensure good impalement/coverage by the electrode array). Allow organoids to equilibrate for ~10 minutes at 37 °C in the chamber or miniature incubator.

Baseline recording Start recording spontaneous activity using your MEA software. Suggested settings (adapt to your system): Sampling rate: ~20 kHz per electrode High-pass filter: ~100 Hz for spike detection Record baseline activity for 10 minutes .

Pharmacological modulation (optional) Carefully add the pharmacological modulator (e.g., 4-AP to a final concentration around 100 µM ) without dislodging the organoids. Continue recording for at least 5 minutes (or longer, depending on experimental design) to capture drug-induced changes in activity.

Block of action potentials (specificity control) At the end of the experiment, add TTX ( Tetrodotoxin ) to the recording chamber at an effective concentration. Record for several minutes to verify suppression of spiking activity and exclude non-neuronal artifacts.

Export raw data or use the MEA software’s spike-detection tools. Apply a spike-detection algorithm of choice (e.g., noise-dependent threshold-based or template-based) Validate detected spikes visually and/or with automated quality metrics or AI-based classifiers in BrainWave5 software, 3Brain, if available. Quantify measures such as: Spike rate per electrode Number of active electrodes Burst frequency and duration Network synchrony metrics
creator: Hariam Raji
